critic_raw:
Issue 1: Seasonal recovery bias (tax-refund season). The comparison period Feb–Apr coincides with tax-refund season. Delinquent borrowers receiving refunds are more likely to make payments regardless of which accounts collectors contact first. This seasonal effect is a plausible full explanation for the 16% gain and is entirely independent of model quality. The board memo makes no attempt to adjust for this well-known collections seasonality.

Issue 2: Contactability population change. The new dialer vendor verified more phone numbers and reduced unreachable accounts. This change altered the distribution of accounts in the worked queue — agents no longer had to waste contacts on unreachable accounts. Average recovered balance per assigned account would rise simply because the denominator now includes fewer dead-end contacts, even if every account were ranked identically.

Issue 3: Treatment fidelity violation via manager overrides. Managers could reorder accounts for hardship cases and recently-promised-to-pay customers. The model's ranking was not enforced as the sole treatment. The effective treatment is a hybrid of model rank and manager judgment, making it impossible to measure the model's contribution in isolation.

Issue 4: Missing same-period counterfactual. The fundamental design flaw is the absence of a concurrent control running the old queue rules during Feb–Apr. Without this, there is no way to know whether the 16% gain would have appeared anyway.
defender_raw: The critique identifies four valid concerns about causal attribution. However, it is worth distinguishing between 'the model has no effect' and 'this evaluation cannot prove the model has an effect.' The latter is true; the former remains unknown. The 16% gain is not noise — it is a real operational outcome that warrants rigorous follow-up. None of the confounds listed imply the model provides zero value; they imply the evaluation design cannot separate model value from other contributors. The appropriate response is a concurrent champion-challenger test, not premature dismissal. Using contactability features in the model is a sound modeling choice — the issue is experimental design, not feature validity.
adjudication_raw: The critic correctly identifies four confounds that prevent causal attribution. The defender makes a valid point about the distinction between 'model has no effect' and 'evaluation cannot prove model effect,' but this distinction does not defend the board memo's actual claim. The memo frames the 16% gain as 'evidence that the model is improving collection efficiency' — which is a causal claim. Given that (a) the board memo's causal claim is unsupported and (b) both sides agree a controlled test is needed, verdict is empirical_test_agreed.
all_issues_raised: Issue 2: contactability population change from dialer switch
Issue 3: treatment fidelity violation via manager overrides

empirical_test.condition: Concurrent champion-challenger randomized queue test within the same calendar period, holding dialer policy constant and tracking all manual overrides by condition
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Recovery per account is similar between model-ranked and legacy-ranked concurrent queues under same seasonal and dialer conditions
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Model-ranked concurrent queue shows materially better recovery than legacy-ranked concurrent queue under same conditions
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Model shows positive effect but smaller than 16%, with the residual gap attributable to identifiable seasonal or dialer factors
